request.question: What year was Sidwell Friends School founded where Roderick H. Cox taught for nearly 40 years?

title: Josh Hart (basketball)
Friends School of Wilmington
Tandem Friends School
J.C. Luning
Newtown Friends School
Media-Providence Friends School
Alexandria Friends School
Roderick H. Cox
Sidwell Friends School
Virginia Beach Friends School
sentences: Josh Hart (born March 6, 1995) is an American basketball player for the Los Angeles Lakers of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
He played college basketball for Villanova.
As a senior at Sidwell Friends School, Hart posted averages 24.3 points, 13.4 rebounds and 2.8 steals per game and was a highly recruited prospect.
In his sophomore season at Villanova, Hart was named the Big East Tournament most outstanding player.
As a junior, he was named to the All-Big East first team and helped Villanova win the national championship in 2016.
Friends School of Wilmington is an 18 months - 8th grade private/independent Quaker school located in Wilmington, North Carolina.
The school was founded in 1995 by the Wilmington (NC) Friends Meeting and has grown from a middle school for 30 students to a school for 190 students.
Friends School is located on two campuses located 2.9 miles apart with five buildings and eight acres of playgrounds, fields, gardens, woods, and a pond.
Tandem Friends School is a coeducational secondary school founded in 1970 in Albemarle County, Virginia, just outside Charlottesville, by educators John Howard and Duncan Alling.
In 1995, it joined the Friends Council on Education, adopting the educational beliefs and practices of the Quakers.
The current head of school is Whitney Thompson.
The Upper School, grades 9-12, has approximately 100 students, while the Middle School, grades 5-8, has approximately 120 students.
The head of the Upper School is Peter Gaines, and the head of the Middle School is Tom O'Connor.
Tandem is located on 279 Tandem Lane.
The mascot is a Quaker, though the original mascot was a badger.
When the mascot was changed in 2015, there was a good deal of resistance from students concerning the fact that though the school is technically Quaker, very few students are Quaker.
Students felt misrepresented, the more so since the badger was a mascot the students had chosen themselves, whereas the Quaker was imposed upon them.
The logo is the historic main building (originally a plantation house and later a Civil War hospital), which is thought by some to be haunted.
John Christian Luning (December 21, 1863 – September 28, 1928) was a mayor of the town of Leesburg, Florida, during the 1890s and Lake County Commissioner.
He would go on to serve the shortest term as Florida's Commissioner of Agriculture for 14 days before going on to serve as State Treasurer of Florida.

He was part of a group of Florida leaders known as the "Three Musketeers" – along with James B. Whitfield (a state treasurer and state attorney general before serving nearly 40 years on the Florida Supreme Court) and William V. Knott (who held various cabinet positions for nearly 40 continuous years before becoming a political appointee) – and had a bright political future before his sudden death in September 1928.
Newtown Friends School is a coeducational prekindergarten through eighth grade Quaker independent school founded in 1948 and located in Bucks County, Pennsylvania.
Though its name and address indicate it is in Newtown Township, it is physically located in Middletown Township.
Newtown Friends' campus adjoins with that of George School.
Media-Providence Friends School is a Quaker school founded as Media Friends School in Media, Pennsylvania in 1876.
Alexandria Friends School (AFS) was a private, non-profit Friends (Quaker) middle and high school (grades 6-12) located at 3830 Seminary Road in Alexandria, Virginia, United States.
It was founded in 2006 by faculty and staff from Thornton Friends School's Alexandria campus, which closed at the end of the 2005-2006 school year.
The school closed in 2012, despite promises to reopen the following year.
Roderick Howard Cox (March 5, 1911 – August 25, 2000) was an American track and field athlete, football player, teacher, and environmentalist.
He was the NCAA champion in the hammer throw in 1933 and also played for the Michigan Wolverines football team from 1930 to 1932.
Cox later served in the United States Navy and attained the rank of commander in the Naval Reserve.
He was a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years.
Sidwell Friends School is a highly selective Quaker school located in Bethesda, Maryland and Washington, D.C., offering pre-kindergarten through secondary school classes.
Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell, its motto is ""Eluceat omnibus lux" " (English: Let the light shine out from all ), alluding to the Quaker concept of inner light.
All Sidwell Friends students attend Quaker meeting for worship weekly, and middle school students begin every day with five minutes of silence.
Virginia Beach Friends School (VBFS) is an independent life-skills and college preparatory day school founded in 1955 under the care of the Virginia Beach Friends Meeting.

Virginia Beach Friends School has more than 200 students enrolled in four divisions – Early School (Cottage, Treehouse, Pre-K and Kindergarten), Lower School (Grades 1-5), Middle School (Grades 6-8), and Upper School (Grades 9-12).

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: What year was Sidwell Friends School founded where Roderick H. Cox taught for nearly 40 years?
hop1_docs: Roderick H. Cox | Roderick Howard Cox (March 5, 1911 – August 25, 2000) was an American track and field athlete, football player, teacher, and environmentalist.  He was the NCAA champion in the hammer throw in 1933 and also played for the Michigan Wolverines football team from 1930 to 1932.  Cox later served in the United States Navy and attained the rank of commander in the Naval Reserve.  He was a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years.

Sidwell Friends School | Sidwell Friends School is a highly selective Quaker school located in Bethesda, Maryland and Washington, D.C., offering pre-kindergarten through secondary school classes.  Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell, its motto is ""Eluceat omnibus lux" " (English: Let the light shine out from all ), alluding to the Quaker concept of inner light.  All Sidwell Friends students attend Quaker meeting for worship weekly, and middle school students begin every day with five minutes of silence.

Roderick Cox (conductor) | Roderick Cox is an African American conductor.  He is currently associate conductor of the Minnesota Orchestra, a position he was appointed to in September 2016 after serving as assistant conductor for a year.

Marvin Chirelstein | Marvin A. Chirelstein (October 8, 1928 - February 16, 2015) was a Professor Emeritus of Law at Columbia Law School, where he taught for nearly 30 years.  He taught a contracts course to first year law students as well as tax and corporate finance courses.  Prior to teaching at Columbia, Professor Chirelstein taught for over 15 years at Yale Law School.  He authored several course books on law.

Joe Cox (American football) | Cox started at Independence High School in Charlotte, North Carolina.  Cox was named to the 2004 "Parade Magazine" All-America Team and "Super Prep" All-Mid Atlantic Team.  He was the North Carolina Gatorade Player of the Year.  Cox was on the "AP" first-team All-State team and was a two-time "Charlotte Observer" Offensive Player of the Year.  He also played in the Shrine Bowl of the Carolinas and impressed scouts by going 31-0 as a starting quarterback.  Cox was a two-time team captain and was the number 7 rated quarterback in the country by "Rivals.com" and Tom Lemming of ESPN.  He set a North Carolina record with 66 touchdown passes while taking his team to its fifth consecutive state championship his senior year.  Cox was named the MVP of the state title game twice.  As a junior, Cox threw for nearly 4,000 yards and over 40 touchdowns.  As a senior, he completed 240 out of 363 passes for over 4,500 yards and only five interceptions.

Roderick Sprague | Roderick Sprague III (February 18, 1933 – August 20, 2012) was a renowned American anthropologist, ethnohistorian and historical archaeologist, and the Emeritus Director of the Laboratory of Anthropology at the University of Idaho in Moscow, where he taught for thirty years.  He had extensive experience in environmental impact research, trade beads, aboriginal burial customs, and the Columbia Basin area.

Priscilla Baird | Priscilla Baird (1828-1904) was a pioneering teacher in Missouri and Illinois and an advocate for girls' education.  She began her career in Shelbyville, Missouri in 1851.  She then taught at Liberty Female College and Lancaster schools before relocating to Illinois during the civil war and teaching at the Springfield High School.  Returning to Missouri, she taught at Ingleside College in Palmyra, Missouri and at Hardin College in Mexico, Missouri.  After completing nearly thirty years of teaching, she founded the Baird College in 1885, where she remained until her retirement in 1897.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Roderick H. Cox — [1] "He was a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years."
- Sidwell Friends School — [2] "Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell,"
- Sidwell Friends School (location/motto context) — [2] "Sidwell Friends School is a highly selective Quaker school located in Bethesda, Maryland"

Ambiguities:
- "Roderick Cox" appears as a different entry: [3] "Roderick Cox is an African American conductor." (distinct from "Roderick H. Cox" in [1])

Bridge / missing fact:
- No missing facts: the passages explicitly link Roderick H. Cox to Sidwell Friends School ([1]) and state the school's founding year ([2]). The required relation (founding year) is present and unambiguous.

Retrieval direction:
- Page: "Sidwell Friends School"
- Page: "Roderick H. Cox"
- Optional disambiguation/search: "Roderick Cox disambiguation" or query "Roderick H. Cox Sidwell Friends School"
Conclusion: Sidwell Friends School was founded in 1883.
response.hop2_query: Roderick H Cox Sidwell Friends School founded
hop2_docs: Roderick H. Cox | Roderick Howard Cox (March 5, 1911 – August 25, 2000) was an American track and field athlete, football player, teacher, and environmentalist.  He was the NCAA champion in the hammer throw in 1933 and also played for the Michigan Wolverines football team from 1930 to 1932.  Cox later served in the United States Navy and attained the rank of commander in the Naval Reserve.  He was a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years.

Sidwell Friends School | Sidwell Friends School is a highly selective Quaker school located in Bethesda, Maryland and Washington, D.C., offering pre-kindergarten through secondary school classes.  Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell, its motto is ""Eluceat omnibus lux" " (English: Let the light shine out from all ), alluding to the Quaker concept of inner light.  All Sidwell Friends students attend Quaker meeting for worship weekly, and middle school students begin every day with five minutes of silence.

Roderick Cox (conductor) | Roderick Cox is an African American conductor.  He is currently associate conductor of the Minnesota Orchestra, a position he was appointed to in September 2016 after serving as assistant conductor for a year.

Cox's Bazar Government High School | Cox's Bazar Government High School is a secondary school in Cox's Bazar, the administrative headquarters of the district in Bangladesh of the same name.  It was founded in 1874.  It is located on the north-east side of "Bir-Sreshtho Ruhul Amin" Stadium.  The area of the school is 23 acres.  It is one of the oldest schools in the country.

Roderick Rijnders | Roderick "Rody" Falesca Renee Trygvae Rijnders (born 1 March 1941) is a retired coxswain from the Netherlands.  He won the silver medal in the coxed pairs at the 1968 Summer Olympics, alongside Hadriaan van Nes and Herman Suselbeek, as well a European bronze in 1956.

Leonard Cox | Leonard Cox (or Coxe) (c. 1495 – c. 1549) was an English humanist, author of the first book in English on rhetoric.  He was a scholar of international reputation who found patronage in Poland, and was friend of Erasmus and Melanchthon.  He was known to contemporaries as a grammarian, rhetorician, poet, and preacher, and was skilled in the modern as well as the classical languages.

Seacology | Seacology is a nonprofit 501(c)(3) charitable organization headquartered in Berkeley, California, that works to preserve island ecosystems and cultures around the world.  Founded in 1991, it began with the work of ethnobotanist Paul Alan Cox, who researched tropical plants and their medicinal value in the village of Falealupo in Samoa during the mid-1980s.  When the villagers were pressured into selling logging rights to their rainforest in 1988 to build a new school, Cox and his wife offered to help secure funds for the new school in return for an agreement with the villagers to protect their forest.  With the help of his friends and family, Cox secured the funds within six months, later earning him and the village chief, Fuiono Senio, the Goldman Environmental Prize for their efforts.  Word spread throughout the islands, and with increasing demand for similar projects, Cox, along with Bill Marré and Ken Murdock, decided to form Seacology and expand their work internationally.  For the first few years, the organization operated on a volunteer basis.  Duane Silverstein became the first employee in 1999, and headquarters were relocated to Berkeley, not far from his residence.
response.summary_2: Roderick H. Cox taught at Sidwell Friends School, which was founded in 1883.
